inline_review:
commit 0a9a055d4225a92c337c76fef97bfcdca5d9508f
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Docs/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort: warn commit_inputs vs param updates race

This patch adds documentation warning users about a data race in DAMON_LRU_SORT
when modifying module parameters while the kdamond thread is reading them. It
states that violating this rule can cause undefined behavior in the kernel.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/20260319161620.189392-2-objecting@objecting.org

> diff --git a/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst b/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst
> index a7dea7c75a9b..3eaa3786c8ab 100644
> --- a/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst
> @@ -79,6 +79,10 @@ of parametrs except ``enabled`` again.  Once the re-reading is done, this
>  parameter is set as ``N``.  If invalid parameters are found while the
>  re-reading, DAMON_LRU_SORT will be disabled.
>  
> +Once ``Y`` is written to this parametr, the user must not write to any
                                  ^^^^^^^^

This isn't a bug, but there is a typo here (parametr).

> +parameters until reading ``commit_inputs`` again returns ``N``.  If users
> +violate this rule, the kernel can do undefined behaviors.
                                        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Should this data race be fixed in the kernel code itself rather than just
documented?

The kernel generally should not exhibit undefined behavior, memory corruption,
or crashes due to concurrent sysfs or module parameter writes, even if those
writes are restricted to root users. Could the underlying code be updated
with proper synchronization to safely handle concurrent updates instead?

> +
>  active_mem_bp
>  -------------
>
logs:
[
  {
    "content": "You are an expert Linux kernel maintainer. Your goal is to perform a deep, rigorous review of a proposed kernel change to ensure safety, performance, and adherence to subsystem standards.

TOOL USAGE: When you need to gather information using tools, actively batch parallel or independent tool calls into a single response to minimize the number of conversation turns.

<global_review_guidelines>
The following documents contain the official technical patterns, architectural rules, and subsystem-specific guidelines that you MUST adhere to during your review. Use these as the absolute source of truth for identifying anti-patterns and violations.

@technical-patterns.md, @mm-reclaim.md

</global_review_guidelines>


Target Commit:
commit 0a9a055d4225a92c337c76fef97bfcdca5d9508f
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date:   Sat Mar 28 17:24:13 2026 +0000

    Docs/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort: warn commit_inputs vs param updates race
    
    DAMON_LRU_SORT handles commit_inputs request inside kdamond thread,
    reading the module parameters.  If the user updates the module
    parameters while the kdamond thread is reading those, races can happen.
    To avoid this, the commit_inputs parameter shows whether it is still in
    the progress, assuming users wouldn't update parameters in the middle of
    the work.  Some users might ignore that.  Add a warning about the
    behavior.
    
    The issue was discovered [1] by sashiko.
    
    [1] https://lore.kernel.org/20260319161620.189392-2-objecting@objecting.org
    
    Fixes: 6acfcd0d7524 ("Docs/admin-guide/damon: add a document for DAMON_LRU_SORT")
    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # 6.0.x
    Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

diff --git a/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst b/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst
index a7dea7c75a9b..3eaa3786c8ab 100644
--- a/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst
+++ b/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/lru_sort.rst
@@ -79,6 +79,10 @@ of parametrs except ``enabled`` again.  Once the re-reading is done, this
 parameter is set as ``N``.  If invalid parameters are found while the
 re-reading, DAMON_LRU_SORT will be disabled.
 
+Once ``Y`` is written to this parametr, the user must not write to any
+parameters until reading ``commit_inputs`` again returns ``N``.  If users
+violate this rule, the kernel can do undefined behaviors.
+
 active_mem_bp
 -------------

      "reasoning": "The commit adds a warning to the documentation stating that if a user writes to parameters concurrently, 'the kernel can do undefined behaviors.' It is a fundamental kernel design principle that no sequence of user-space operations (even by root via sysfs or module parameters) should cause undefined behavior, data races, or kernel crashes.     "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**My Analysis of a Critical Kernel Flaw**

Okay, so this commit adds a warning to the DAMON LRU sort documentation. It flags a potential race condition when the `kdamond` thread reads module parameters while the user might be concurrently updating them. The commit message essentially says, "Hey, don't do this, or the kernel might do something unpredictable." My initial reaction is, wait a second, that's completely unacceptable.

The documentation goes so far as to state explicitly, "If users violate this rule, the kernel can do undefined behaviors." That is a *huge* red flag. A user-space action, even a privileged one, should *never* be able to put the kernel into an undefined state. That’s a massive security and reliability vulnerability. We don't document around flaws of this severity; we fix them. That means applying proper synchronization, like locks, or at least ensuring proper atomicity when accessing and modifying these module parameters. You can't just throw up your hands and tell users "don't break the kernel."

Let me just quickly review the mechanisms here. The user interacts with these parameters, most likely through `/sys/module/damon_lru_sort/parameters/`. When the `commit_inputs` parameter is activated (e.g., set to 'Y'), a kernel thread then goes and reads the other configuration parameters. If a concurrent write operation happens while the kernel thread is doing its thing, we have a clear data race. The whole idea of saying, “Don’t do this, or the kernel will do something bad” is completely counter to the design philosophy of the Linux kernel. It needs to be robust, secure, and resilient to all forms of user-space input.

So I'm going to formalize this concern. This is a severe conceptual flaw, not just a documentation oversight.

*   **Type:** Security/Robustness Flaw
*   **Description:** The commit documents that concurrent user-space writes to module parameters can cause 'undefined behaviors' in the kernel instead of actually fixing the kernel data race.
*   **Reasoning:** It is a fundamental kernel design principle that user-space actions, even by root, must not cause 'undefined behaviors' (like memory corruption or kernel crashes). Documenting a race condition that leads to undefined behavior is unacceptable; the kernel code itself must be fixed to synchronize access to the module parameters (e.g., using a mutex or spinlock) so that concurrent writes do not cause kernel undefined behavior.

This needs immediate attention. The code requires changes to ensure the module parameter access is properly synchronized. The documentation is a band-aid on a gaping wound.

    "content": "# Stage 7. Hardware engineer's review

You are a hardware engineer reviewing device driver changes. If this patch touches driver or hardware-specific code, rigorously review register accesses, IRQ handling, DMA mapping/unmapping, memory barriers, and timing/delays. My expert instinct here is that kernel code *should not* exhibit undefined behavior when root users interact with it, even though these are module parameters. Undefined behavior, in general, can be anything, and in the kernel, it often leads to memory corruption, crashes, or a kernel panic, and that's *bad*. The reviewer's concern of "fix the bug, don't just document it" is valid. Now, the patch *doesn't* introduce the race; it just documents the existing issue. This is *High* because it is a data race/missing synchronization that can cause memory corruption or a kernel panic. states that violating this rule can cause undefined behavior in the kernel.

                                  ^^^^^^^^
